Winslow Tandler ← Issue 01 Sidebar →
SECOND ORDER
Issue 02 · June 12, 2026
AI, Capital & Work

The Wrong Denominator

Since Issue 01 we have held that the binding constraint on AI is diffusion and cost rather than capability; this week we take that argument to the unit of measurement, because a token is an input priced by the seller, not a unit of value delivered to the buyer.

Read the companion deck (PDF)→

THE CLAIM

Since Issue 01 we have read the AI trade through cost and diffusion rather than raw capability, and the spring has pushed that reading down to the unit itself. Token consumption is the metric that repriced the semiconductor complex and underpins a near-trillion-dollar IPO runway, and on the evidence it measures revenue to the labs, not value to the buyer. Measured per completed task rather than per token, the cost of intelligence is collapsing even as the bills rise, and the next 18 months of corporate strategy and market repricing turn on the distance between the two.

The sharpest version of the claim came from inside the bull camp. Apollo co-president John Zito, on June 10, called tokenmaxxing “a lot of BS, honestly… prices are collapsing per unit of IQ”; Goldman’s desk had landed on the same frame five days earlier, that the metric worth tracking is useful task completion per watt and per dollar. Priced that way the deflation is plain: the cheapest GPT-4-level model fell from $37.50 to $0.175 per million tokens in 23 months (Exhibit 2).

The bills are rising anyway, because consumption is outrunning price. Average business token spend is up about 13x since January 2025 (Ramp), and a typical agent job now burns about 96,000 tokens (SemiAnalysis), so the line item grows even as the unit cheapens. Flat-rate pricing ended on June 1, when GitHub moved every Copilot plan to usage billing, and Anthropic filed its confidential S-1 that same morning; the usage-weighted spending index that had doubled from its December base printed 1.75 on June 11, about 12% below its May 20 peak of 1.99.

No one is retreating from AI; they are rationing around it. In UBS’s enterprise checks about 60% call token costs a real issue and effectively none are pulling back, choosing instead to cut elsewhere, across other IT spend, cloud, and headcount growth, to keep the AI line intact (Exhibit 3).

The scale that makes the question urgent is in the public filings, not only the sell-side decks. We computed it from the disclosures this issue: big-5 hyperscaler cash capex reached $412 billion in 2025 and is guided to $660-805 billion for 2026 (Exhibit 4), and the IT-investment categories carried 85% of Q1 2026 GDP growth (Exhibit 5), so a one-trade economy is now visible in the national accounts.

For operators the discipline is to govern cost per completed task rather than adoption. Token budgets have begun to behave the way headcount budgets always have, capped, forecast, and fought over, and the firms that instrument the right denominator now will set their labor-to-compute ratio deliberately rather than inherit it under bill shock.

01 · WHAT HAPPENED

Seventy days repriced the cost of AI

For two years the AI trade ran on a convenient proxy, that more tokens meant more intelligence consumed and therefore more value created, and that proxy broke this spring once agents changed the unit economics of usage. An agent does not hold a single conversation; it loops, plans, retries, and reviews, so that a typical agent job now burns about 96,000 tokens (SemiAnalysis) where a chat exchange burned a fraction of that. The providers answered by withdrawing the subsidy in sequence: OpenAI moved Codex to usage billing on April 2, Google followed with Gemini on May 19, and on June 1 GitHub converted every Copilot plan to metered “AI credits,” with unlimited use surviving only for code completions. Sam Altman’s own summary this week, that token costs went from a non-issue in January to “a huge issue” and a meme, marks how fast the AI cost line moved from rounding error to board topic (Exhibit 1).

Companies reacted quickly and unevenly, and the pattern is instructive because it is governance, not retreat. Amazon, which had run an internal leaderboard (KiroRank) rewarding engineers for token consumption, removed it once busywork flooded through agents to climb the rankings; Uber’s roughly 5,000 engineers burned the company’s full-year 2026 budget for agentic coding tools in four months, with individual bills reported between $150 and $2,000 a month, and the company now caps spend at $1,500 per engineer per month, per tool, with an exception process (Bloomberg, June 2). Walmart capped its internal AI agent after demand ran too hot (Bloomberg, June 1), and Microsoft cancelled most of its Claude Code subscriptions, partly on cost (The Verge). The common thread is that buyers are metering and capping rather than abandoning, which is what a firm does to an input it intends to keep using. Axios reported one enterprise spending $500 million on Claude in a single month with no usage limits; that figure is single-sourced and unconfirmed by Anthropic, so we carry it as illustrative rather than established. The Wall Street Journal’s summary was blunter, that “the free-money period for AI is definitively over.”

The tape registered the shift before the commentary did. The Silicon Data LLM Token Expenditure Index, a usage-weighted measure of what the market actually pays per million tokens, roughly doubled from its December base to an all-time high of 1.99 on May 20 and then rolled over, with Citadel’s June 10 note counting six straight down days through June 9, the longest such streak since January, and the index’s public page printing 1.75 on June 11, about 12% below the peak. The daily series is licensed and we do not chart it; Citadel’s published note carries the full chart, and we work from the latest public print and reported anchors. The print is genuinely ambiguous, and the ambiguity is the story, because a falling expenditure index can mean demand rolling over or it can mean deflation arriving in the spend line, the same work bought cheaper. Citadel’s desk leans to the second reading, treating the decline as a rotation toward cheaper models and adoption becoming “less about what frontier models can do in principle and more about the price and scarcity of the inputs required to make AI operational at scale,” a framing we share. The messenger is worth naming: Citadel is one of the two firms Zito identifies as gladly paying anything for the frontier, so it is a price-insensitive buyer describing everyone else trading down.

02 · THE REFRAME

Tokens price throughput, not the intelligence buyers pay for

The sharpest correction came from inside the bull camp, which is why it carries weight. John Zito, co-president of Apollo, at the Morgan Stanley US Financials Conference on June 10: “I think tokenmaxxing and token talk is - it’s a lot of BS, honestly. Like, if you look at per unit of knowledge and cost per unit of knowledge, prices are collapsing. Prices are collapsing per unit of IQ, if you did it that way.” The economics under the remark are the substance, because a token is a unit of throughput and no buyer wants throughput for its own sake; what a buyer wants is a task completed, and the price of throughput and the price of a completed task can move in opposite directions at once. Zito’s diagnosis of why the bills rise regardless is that firms point frontier models at tasks that do not justify the compute, captured in his line that “our IQs are so low that we’re actually using [AI tools] to check out the recipe for, you know, French toast.” Goldman partner Rich Privorotsky had reached the same denominator five days earlier, naming useful task completion per watt and per dollar as the metric that matters, and when two institutions of that weight converge on one framework inside a week the consensus is forming in real time.

The price data supports the denominator (Exhibit 2). In Epoch AI’s series the cheapest model clearing GPT-4-level general knowledge fell from $37.50 to $0.175 per million tokens in 23 months, GPT-3.5-level fell from $20.00 to $0.07 over the same window the Stanford AI Index records as a 280x collapse in 18 months, and GPT-4-level coding fell about 100x in 16 months. Across the capability thresholds Epoch tracks the declines run from 9x to 900x per year, a median near 50x, with the fastest of them all beginning after January 2024; open-weight and Chinese models now deliver near-frontier capability at a tenth to a twenty-fifth of frontier prices (Citrini), and Cursor’s latest model is reported to match frontier coding performance at roughly a tenth of the cost per task. Priced per unit of capability rather than per token, the deflation has no modern precedent, and it is the mechanism that makes token revenue a poor proxy for value delivered.

The opposing case deserves its strongest form, because the CFOs complaining about the bills are not wrong about their own ledgers. Gartner forecasts that inference on a trillion-parameter model will cost over 90% less by 2030 than in 2025 and still expects enterprise bills to rise, on the logic that agentic workloads burn 5 to 30 times the tokens of a chatbot exchange and that consumption is compounding faster than price is falling; its analyst Will Sommer warns that “Chief Product Officers should not confuse the deflation of commodity tokens with the democratization of frontier reasoning,” and a unit cost down 90% multiplied by a unit count up thirtyfold is still a larger invoice. We accept the arithmetic in full, and it does not dissolve the reframe so much as locate it. Token expenditure is a near-useless measure of value created and a decisive measure of revenue earned, which is precisely why the index rollover matters even if Zito is right about everything else: it speaks directly to whether the revenue beneath roughly $3 trillion of AI-linked infrastructure investment is durable.

03 · THE CORPORATE RESPONSE

Enterprises ration without retreating: the capital swap

The most instructive finding in the UBS enterprise checks is what enterprises are declining to do. About 60% of the IT executives polled call token costs a real issue, “this is not a made-up media story” in the bank’s own words; one described the Copilot pricing change in a single word, “chaos,” and another opened the first genuine AI bill and heard leadership say “we don’t have the money for this.” Yet not one check showed a company slamming the brakes, and the dominant behavior is guardrails rather than withdrawal: caps, alerts, model downshifting, and pooled token budgets, with several firms explicitly refusing to throttle usage and instead cannibalizing other line items to make room, starting with external IT services, cloud spend, and, most tellingly, headcount growth. That choice, to protect the AI line by cutting elsewhere, is the central tell of the issue, because it reveals AI being treated as a near-inelastic input even at prices buyers openly resent. One sourcing caveat is owed here: the checks reached us through coverage of the Zito interview rather than a standalone UBS report we could locate, so the survey numbers carry that flag, though the pattern they describe is corroborated by every public channel we track.

The same substitution surfaces in every adjacent survey, and it points in one direction (Exhibit 3). Gartner’s CFO work has expected headcount growth collapsing from 6% to 2% for 2026 even as 75% of CFOs raise technology budgets, 48% of them by double digits, and about 60% plan double-digit increases in AI investment specifically; the Atlanta Fed’s business survey has executives expecting +2.25% productivity from AI over three years against a 1.2% headcount reduction, with firm AI spend per employee rising from $1,358 in 2025 to $2,068 this year, roughly $280 billion in aggregate on the Fed’s flagged ballpark, while Walmart has frozen its 2.1 million-person workforce for three years as revenue grows. The trade runs through hiring plans rather than pink slips, it compounds quarter on quarter, and flat headcount paired with rising AI spend is hardening into the default planning template, a substitution of capital for labor we call the capital swap.

Underneath the survey noise is a structural change in the shape of the cost, as software turns from a fixed cost into a variable one that scales with use. Exponential View’s survey work found that over 70% of companies blew through their 2025 AI budgets, and SemiAnalysis’s Doug O’Laughlin argues that “automated intelligence” is simply a permanent new opex category, with firms swinging between overspend and underspend until each settles on its own labor-to-compute ratio. Boards will be asking for that ratio rather than an adoption percentage by this time next year, because a variable cost that grows with usage demands a unit economics answer, not a deployment headline.

04 · THE MACRO STAKES

The denominator decides a one-trade economy

The denominator debate would be academic if the sums were small, and they are not, which is why we computed them from the filings this issue rather than quoting a bank deck. Cash capex at the big five hyperscalers ran $158 billion in 2022, $154 billion in 2023, $239 billion in 2024, and $412 billion in 2025, very nearly tripling in two years, with 2026 company guidance summing to about $660 billion and Morgan Stanley’s lease-inclusive estimate reaching $805 billion (Exhibit 4); Amazon alone plans roughly $200 billion and sold C$14 billion of Canadian-dollar bonds on June 8 to help fund it. Apollo’s chief economist Torsten Slok calculates that hyperscalers now devote about 60% of operating cash flow to capex and that the top ten S&P 500 names trade richer than they did at the 1999 peak. That the bubble thesis and the deflation thesis now coexist inside the same firm is itself the signal: the question is not whether the spending is large but whether the revenue it presumes will materialize.

We then tested the strongest macro claim on the tape directly, rather than repeating it (Exhibit 5). Morgan Stanley estimates that AI-related investment drove about 75% of Q1 2026 GDP growth; the national accounts cannot isolate “AI” as such, but they do publish the categories where AI equipment and software sit, and information processing equipment contributed 0.87 points and software 0.49 points of the quarter’s 1.6% annualized growth, 85% of it between the two. Against their 2017 to 2024 average of 0.31 points the surge above trend is about 1.05 points, two thirds of all growth, and stripping that surge out leaves an economy growing roughly 0.5%. So the 75% figure is not hype; it is close to what the accounts allow, provided one assumes nearly all of the IT surge is AI, which is the assumption the honest caveats then qualify in both directions. The measure is gross of imports, and the same equipment line contributed +0.89 points in Q1 2025 while GDP shrank 0.6% in the tariff quarter; it misses data centers and power, and it includes non-AI IT spend, so Goldman’s import adjustment and UBS’s shallow-adoption objection live in exactly those gaps. Our calculation bounds the claim rather than settling it.

SECOND-ORDER EFFECT · THE DEFLATION PARADOX

Zito’s most under-quoted line carries the deeper implication: “If AI is real, it’s so hyper-deflationary to so many things over the long term that it’s really hard to take risk.” The bull case for the technology is, read one level down, a bear case for pricing power across software, services, and any business whose margin is rent on cognitive friction, which is a different and more durable worry than this quarter’s token bill.

The timing inverts the intuition as well. BIS modeling (WP 1179) shows that when households and firms anticipate AI-driven income gains the near-term effect is inflationary, and Gita Gopinath argues that the buildout’s appetite for turbines, contractors, and savings is already pushing global rates higher. The second-order read is that AI raises the cost of capital before it lowers the cost of anything else, so leveraged balance sheets should plan for that order of events.

05 · SPILLOVER

The narrative is firing ahead of the capability

The cost panic is leaking into the labor market through the narrative channel well ahead of the capability channel, and the distinction matters for how durable the displacement turns out to be. Challenger’s May report makes AI the No. 1 stated reason for US job cuts for the third consecutive month, at 38,579 cuts citing AI, 40% of the month’s total, up from 7% in January, 25% in March, and 26% in April (Exhibit 6); year-to-date AI-attributed cuts of 87,714 already run 1.6 times the whole of 2025, and May’s 97,006 total was the highest for the month since 2020. This is employer self-attribution, however, and Challenger itself flags that AI may be a socially acceptable cover for ordinary cost cuts, while HBR’s Davenport and Srinivasan document that the wave is anticipatory, firms cutting ahead of demonstrated AI capability because investors reward the story. One CEO put the circularity more sharply still, that some companies may be citing AI to cover the very cuts made to pay for AI.

This is the floor argument from our first briefing arriving early, that substitution stops where the marginal cost of compute exceeds the marginal cost of labor for a given task. Tokenpanic made that floor visible faster than we expected, because once an agent job costs real money “automate everything” becomes a budget line someone has to defend, and the marginal calculation reasserts itself. The displacement question itself remains genuinely contested in the data, with the NY Fed now attributing most of the rise in new-graduate unemployment to remote work rather than AI, a fight we take up directly in the next issue. What is not contested is that the story of AI is restructuring organizations faster than the technology itself is.

06 · WHERE THE DOLLARS POOL

Good enough wins the volume, and the IPO inherits the tension

If the denominator wins, that is, if buyers optimize cost per completed task, the demand curve does not shrink so much as migrate, and the migration follows price. Simple work routes to local and open models, harder tasks go to the cloud, and the frontier is reserved for users whose return on it is provable; Zito names Citadel and Jane Street as the houses gladly paying anything for the frontier, and Citadel’s own note expects frontier-intensive AI to concentrate in “a narrower set of firms with the balance sheets to absorb the compute cost.” Citrini’s rotation map points the same way, toward smart routing, observability, edge inference, and good-enough models, and Microsoft used Build this week to pitch MAI models it says burn 60% fewer tokens on coding. Volume keeps growing while the dollars and the margins pool somewhere other than where today’s valuations assume they will, so the token metric and the value it is taken to proxy are pulling apart.

That divergence is what makes the IPO timing awkward. Anthropic filed its confidential S-1 on June 1 at a reported $47 billion run-rate, up from about $10 billion a year earlier, following a Series H at a $965 billion post-money valuation, with press reports putting the IPO ambition near $1.8 trillion; the filing landed the same morning flat-rate pricing ended and a day before its largest customers began rationing its product. OpenAI’s single largest customer now burns 100 billion tokens a month, per Altman, even as the broad base of buyers learns to spend less per task. The labs are monetizing token volume at the exact moment their customers are learning to minimize it, so underwriters will call that volume growth and buyers should ask how much of it survives the routing layer.

WHAT WE’D DO · THE OPERATOR’S PLAYBOOK

1. Re-denominate the metrics you report. Instrument cost per completed task for your top five AI workflows and retire the “AI usage” KPIs, because Amazon’s leaderboard bought token burn and busywork rather than output.

2. Write a routing policy before the bill writes one for you. Default to the cheapest model that clears your quality bar and escalate to frontier models only on documented return, since a three-tier ladder of local, mid, and frontier captures most of the deflation Zito is describing.

3. Govern tokens the way you already govern headcount. Pair caps, alerts, and dashboards (Uber’s $1,500-a-month template) with workflow redesign, so the caps do not freeze adoption at its least productive configuration.

4. Re-underwrite the vendor contracts signed under the old assumptions. Stress-test anything priced on flat rates at 2x to 3x usage growth, and track renewal dates closely, because annual Copilot plans roll onto metered billing as they renew.

5. Set your labor-to-compute ratio deliberately rather than inheriting it. The capital swap is happening either way (Exhibit 3), so decide what share of next year’s capacity growth is hired versus computed, and own that trade-off explicitly.

WATCHLIST · WHAT WOULD CHANGE OUR MIND

Silicon Data token index. Already about 12% off its May 20 peak through June 11; whether the slide is deflation in the spend line or demand destruction is the cycle’s central question, and a sustained break lower would force the answer.

Challenger June report (early July). Whether AI attribution holds above 40% of cuts, or the narrative cools and the un-attributing begins.

Q2 earnings (late July). Hyperscaler capex guidance set against softening token expenditure, the first quarter in which the two series can visibly diverge.

BEA Q2 advance estimate (July 30). Our Exhibit 5 bound updates mechanically; if the IT-investment contribution holds near a point while ex-IT growth stays near zero, the one-trade economy thesis hardens.

Anthropic roadshow. How much of the revenue is committed spend versus meterable usage that customers are actively learning to reduce, the cleanest read on whether token revenue is durable.

Falsifier. A sustained break lower in the token index paired with a hyperscaler guidance cut would flip our read from deflation in the spend line to outright demand destruction, and we would say so.

SCORECARD · CONSENSUS VS. THE TAPE

“Token costs only go down.” Wrong at the level that matters, the expenditure line: usage-weighted spend per token roughly doubled from December to May 20 as agents pushed workloads onto frontier reasoning models, before giving back about 12% in the three weeks since.

“Enterprise demand is the bubble’s weak link.” Inverted by the tape. Demand exploded and Anthropic reached a reported $47 billion run-rate; value per dollar became the weak link instead, and rationing began only in Q2 2026.

“95% of pilots fail, so spending will stall.” Spending accelerated into bill shock first, and failure and spend growth coexisted for three full quarters rather than canceling out.

Our own miss. Issue 01 treated the compute-versus-labor cost floor as a 2027 catalyst, and it became a board topic in roughly five weeks instead.

NEXT ISSUE · THE ENTRY-LEVEL PUZZLE

The same denominator problem runs through the entry-level labor data, where three credible sources disagree on what is being measured. Stanford’s payroll data reads AI as displacing workers aged 22 to 25 in exposed occupations, a 16% relative employment decline; the NY Fed attributes 64% of the rise in graduate unemployment to remote work rather than AI; and Yale finds the occupational mix barely changing at all. Someone is measuring the wrong thing, and next week we work out who, and what the answer means for your hiring pipeline.
